title: Getting started with forrest
text: forrest creates publication-ready forest plots with a single dependency (tinyplot) and a clean, minimal API. It works with base R data frames, tibbles, and data.tables.

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Basic forest plot
The simplest call requires only three column names: estimate, lower, and upper.

dat <- data.frame(
  study    = c("Smith 2018", "Jones 2019", "Brown 2020",
               "Garcia 2021", "Lee 2022"),
  estimate = c(-0.18, 0.05, -0.12, -0.08, 0.02),
  lower    = c(-0.38, -0.12, -0.30, -0.24, -0.14),
  upper    = c(0.02, 0.22, 0.06, 0.08, 0.18)
)

forrest(
  dat,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "lower",
  upper    = "upper",
  label    = "study",
  xlab     = "Mean difference (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Adding a summary (pooled) estimate
Mark one or more rows as summary estimates with an is_summary logical column. Those rows are drawn as filled diamonds instead of squares.

dat$is_pooled <- c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
dat <- rbind(dat, data.frame(
  study      = "Pooled",
  estimate   = -0.07,
  lower      = -0.16,
  upper      =  0.02,
  is_pooled  = TRUE
))

forrest(
  dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_pooled",
  xlab       = "Mean difference (95% CI)",
  title      = "Association with outcome"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Log scale for ratio measures
When plotting odds ratios, hazard ratios, or risk ratios, set log_scale = TRUE and ref_line = 1.

or_dat <- data.frame(
  study    = c("Adams 2016", "Baker 2018", "Chen 2019",
               "Davis 2020", "Evans 2021", "Pooled"),
  estimate = c(1.45, 0.88, 1.21, 1.07, 0.95, 1.09),
  lower    = c(1.10, 0.65, 0.92, 0.80, 0.72, 0.94),
  upper    = c(1.91, 1.19, 1.59, 1.43, 1.25, 1.26),
  is_sum   = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

forrest(
  or_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  xlab       = "Odds ratio (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Subgroup headers and spacer rows
Rows whose estimate is NA are treated as visual separators. A non-empty label is rendered in bold as a subgroup header; an empty label creates a blank spacer line.

sub_dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c(
    "Sex",
    "  Female", "  Male", "  Pooled",
    "",
    "Age group",
    "  18‚Äď44 years", "  45‚Äď64 years", "  65+ years", "  Pooled",
    "",
    "Overall"
  ),
  estimate = c(
    NA,  -0.10, -0.05, -0.07,
    NA,
    NA,  -0.12, -0.06, -0.02, -0.07,
    NA,
    -0.07
  ),
  lower    = c(
    NA,  -0.22, -0.18, -0.16,
    NA,
    NA,  -0.28, -0.18, -0.12, -0.16,
    NA,
    -0.16
  ),
  upper    = c(
    NA,  0.02, 0.08, 0.02,
    NA,
    NA,  0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.02,
    NA,
    0.02
  ),
  is_sum   = c(
    FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,
    FALSE,
    FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,
    FALSE,
    TRUE
  )
)

forrest(
  sub_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  xlab       = "Mean difference (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Group coloring
Pass a group column to color estimates by a categorical variable. A legend is added automatically.

grp_dat <- data.frame(
  study    = c("Smith 2018", "Jones 2019", "Brown 2020",
               "Garcia 2021", "Lee 2022", "Kim 2023"),
  estimate = c(-0.18, 0.05, -0.12, 0.22, -0.08, 0.15),
  lower    = c(-0.38, -0.12, -0.30,  0.04, -0.24, -0.01),
  upper    = c(0.02, 0.22, 0.06,  0.40, 0.08, 0.31),
  region   = c("Europe", "Europe", "Europe",
               "Americas", "Americas", "Americas")
)

forrest(
  grp_dat,
  estimate = "estimate",
  lower    = "lower",
  upper    = "upper",
  label    = "study",
  group    = "region",
  xlab     = "Mean difference (95% CI)"
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Adding text columns
Use the cols argument ‚ÄĒ a named character vector mapping display headers to column names ‚ÄĒ to show statistics alongside the plot. This triggers a multi-panel layout with a label panel on the left and text columns on the right.

or_dat$or_text <- c(
  "1.45 (1.10, 1.91)",
  "0.88 (0.65, 1.19)",
  "1.21 (0.92, 1.59)",
  "1.07 (0.80, 1.43)",
  "0.95 (0.72, 1.25)",
  "1.09 (0.94, 1.26)"
)
or_dat$pval <- c("0.009", "0.372", "0.175", "0.647", "0.676", "<0.001")

forrest(
  or_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  header     = "Study",
  cols       = c("OR (95% CI)" = "or_text", "P-value" = "pval"),
  widths     = c(2, 4, 2, 1.2)
)

title: Getting started with forrest
text:
Study weights
Pass weight as a column name of numeric study weights (e.g.¬†inverse-variance weights). Point size scales proportionally to sqrt(weight / max(weight)), so larger studies appear with a bigger marker ‚ÄĒ a convention in meta-analysis forest plots.

set.seed(42)
wt_dat <- data.frame(
  study    = paste("Study", LETTERS[1:7]),
  estimate = c(-0.20, 0.04, -0.09, -0.15, 0.03, -0.08, -0.07),
  lower    = c(-0.38, -0.10, -0.24, -0.32, -0.11, -0.22, -0.16),
  upper    = c(-0.02, 0.18, 0.06, 0.02, 0.17, 0.06, 0.02),
  weight   = c(12.1, 4.5, 9.8, 7.2, 5.3, 11.0, 50.1),
  is_sum   = c(rep(FALSE, 6), TRUE)
)

forrest(
  wt_dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  weight     = "weight",
  xlab       = "Mean difference (95% CI)"
)

title: forrest
text:
forrest creates publication-ready forest plots with a single dependency ‚ÄĒ tinyplot. It is designed for epidemiologists and data scientists who need clean, customisable forest plots without pulling in a large dependency tree.
Key features:

Minimal dependency footprint (only tinyplot)
Subgroup headers and spacer rows via NA estimate rows
Summary estimates rendered as filled diamonds
Study weights scale point size (meta-analysis convention)
Confidence intervals clipped at axis limits with directional arrows
Group coloring with automatic Okabe-Ito legend
Optional text columns (OR, p-value, ‚Ä¶) alongside the plot
Log-scale x-axis for ratio measures
Works with data.frame, tibble, and data.table



# Development version from GitHub:
# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("lorenzoFabbri/forrest")



library(forrest)

dat <- data.frame(
  study = c(
    "Smith 2018", "Jones 2019", "Brown 2020",
    "Garcia 2021", "Lee 2022", "Pooled"
  ),
  estimate = c(-0.18, 0.05, -0.12, -0.08, 0.02, -0.07),
  lower = c(-0.38, -0.12, -0.30, -0.24, -0.14, -0.16),
  upper = c(0.02, 0.22, 0.06, 0.08, 0.18, 0.02),
  is_sum = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE),
  weight = c(12.1, 4.5, 9.8, 7.2, 5.3, NA)
)

forrest(
  dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  weight     = "weight",
  xlab       = "Mean difference (95% CI)"
)




sub <- data.frame(
  label = c(
    "Sex", "  Female", "  Male", "  Pooled",
    "", "Region", "  North", "  South", "  Pooled"
  ),
  est = c(NA, -0.10, -0.05, -0.07, NA, NA, -0.12, -0.02, -0.07),
  lo = c(NA, -0.22, -0.18, -0.16, NA, NA, -0.28, -0.12, -0.16),
  hi = c(NA, 0.02, 0.08, 0.02, NA, NA, 0.04, 0.08, 0.02),
  is_sum = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE),
  or_text = c(
    "", "-0.10 (-0.22, 0.02)", "-0.05 (-0.18, 0.08)",
    "-0.07 (-0.16, 0.02)", "",
    "", "-0.12 (-0.28, 0.04)", "-0.02 (-0.12, 0.08)",
    "-0.07 (-0.16, 0.02)"
  )
)

forrest(
  sub,
  estimate   = "est",
  lower      = "lo",
  upper      = "hi",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  header     = "Subgroup",
  cols       = c("Estimate (95% CI)" = "or_text"),
  widths     = c(2.2, 4, 2.5)
)




or_dat <- data.frame(
  study  = c("Adams 2016", "Baker 2018", "Chen 2019", "Pooled"),
  est    = c(1.45, 0.88, 1.21, 1.09),
  lo     = c(1.10, 0.65, 0.92, 0.94),
  hi     = c(1.91, 1.19, 1.59, 1.26),
  is_sum = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

forrest(
  or_dat,
  estimate   = "est",
  lower      = "lo",
  upper      = "hi",
  label      = "study",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  ref_line   = 1,
  xlab       = "Odds ratio (95% CI)"
)

library(forrest)

dat <- data.frame(
  study = c(
    "Smith 2018", "Jones 2019", "Brown 2020",
    "Garcia 2021", "Lee 2022", "Pooled"
  ),
  estimate = c(-0.18, 0.05, -0.12, -0.08, 0.02, -0.07),

  upper      = "upper",

title: forrest 0.1.0
text:
forrest 0.1.0

Initial release.
forrest() creates publication-ready forest plots with support for subgroup headers, summary estimates (diamonds), grouped color mapping, and optional text columns alongside the plot.
forrest() gains a weight argument to scale point size proportionally to study weights ‚ÄĒ the conventional meta-analysis display.
Confidence intervals that extend beyond xlim are now clipped at the axis boundary; a directional arrow indicates the truncated side.

title: forrest
text:
Draws a publication-ready forest plot from a data frame. Each row represents one study, subgroup, or estimate. Rows whose estimate is NA are treated as spacer/header rows: they produce no point or confidence interval, and their label is rendered in bold if non-empty.



forrest(
  data,
  estimate,
  lower,
  upper,
  label = NULL,
  group = NULL,
  is_summary = NULL,
  weight = NULL,
  ref_line = 0,
  log_scale = FALSE,
  xlim = NULL,
  xlab = "Estimate (95% CI)",
  title = NULL,
  header = NULL,
  cols = NULL,
  widths = NULL,
  pch = 15,
  lwd = 2,
  cex = 1,
  col = NULL,
  legend_pos = "topright",
  ...
)







data


A data frame, tibble, or data.table.




estimate


Column name (string) for point estimates.




lower


Column name (string) for lower confidence interval bounds.




upper


Column name (string) for upper confidence interval bounds.




label


Column name (string) for row labels displayed on the y-axis or in the left text panel. If NULL, row numbers are used.




group


Column name (string) for a grouping variable. Rows that share a group value receive the same color, and a legend is drawn automatically.




is_summary


Column name (string) of a logical vector. Rows where this is TRUE are drawn as filled diamonds (pooled / summary estimates) rather than squares with whiskers.




weight


Column name (string) of numeric study weights (e.g. inverse-variance weights or effective sample sizes). When provided, point size scales as cex * sqrt(weight / max(weight)), so larger studies are displayed with a bigger marker ‚ÄĒ the conventional meta-analysis convention. Weights for summary rows are ignored (diamond size is controlled by cex).




ref_line


Numeric. Position of the vertical reference line (e.g.¬†0 for log-odds / risk differences, 1 for odds ratios on the natural scale). Set to NULL to suppress. Default is 0.




log_scale


Logical. If TRUE, apply a log transformation to the x-axis. Useful when plotting odds ratios, hazard ratios, or risk ratios on the natural scale. Default is FALSE.




xlim


Numeric vector of length 2 giving x-axis limits. Computed from the data when NULL (default). Confidence intervals that extend beyond xlim are clipped at the axis boundary and an arrow is drawn to indicate truncation.




xlab


Label for the x-axis. Default is ‚ÄúEstimate (95% CI)‚ÄĚ.




title


Plot title. Default is NULL (no title).




header


Optional header string placed above the label column. When cols is provided this appears above the left text panel; otherwise it is drawn above the topmost row on the y-axis.




cols


Named character vector specifying extra text columns to display to the right of the plot. Names become column headers; values are column names in data. Example: cols = c(‚ÄúOR (95% CI)‚ÄĚ = ‚Äúor_text‚ÄĚ, ‚ÄúP‚ÄĚ = ‚Äúpval_text‚ÄĚ).




widths


Numeric vector of relative panel widths. When cols is NULL, ignored. Otherwise, length must equal 2 + length(cols): label panel, plot panel, then one entry per extra column. Sensible defaults are chosen automatically.




pch


Point character for non-summary rows. Default is 15 (filled square), which is conventional for forest plots.




lwd


Line width for confidence interval whiskers. Default is 2.




cex


Point size multiplier. Default is 1.




col


Color or character vector of colors. When NULL (default) and group is specified, the Okabe-Ito colorblind-safe palette is used. When NULL and no group, a single dark color is used.




legend_pos


Position of the legend when group is supplied. Passed to legend(). Set to NULL to suppress the legend. Default is ‚Äútopright‚ÄĚ.




‚Ä¶


Additional graphical parameters passed to the internal tinyplot::tinyplot() call that sets up the main plot area (e.g. cex.axis, cex.lab, font.main).






Invisibly returns NULL. Called for its side effect of producing a plot.




library("forrest")

# Basic forest plot
dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Pooled"),
  estimate = c(-0.15, 0.10, -0.05, -0.05),
  lower    = c(-0.40, -0.10, -0.25, -0.20),
  upper    = c(0.10, 0.30, 0.15, 0.10),
  is_sum   = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)
forrest(dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum"
)







# With subgroup headers (NA estimate rows) and text columns
dat2 <- data.frame(
  label    = c(
    "Subgroup A", "Study 1", "Study 2", "Pooled A",
    "", "Subgroup B", "Study 3", "Study 4", "Pooled B"
  ),
  estimate = c(NA, 0.5, 0.7, 0.6, NA, NA, 1.1, 0.9, 1.0),
  lower    = c(NA, 0.3, 0.5, 0.45, NA, NA, 0.8, 0.6, 0.75),
  upper    = c(NA, 0.7, 0.9, 0.75, NA, NA, 1.4, 1.2, 1.25),
  is_sum   = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE),
  or_ci    = c(
    "", "0.50 (0.30-0.70)", "0.70 (0.50-0.90)", "0.60 (0.45-0.75)",
    "", "", "1.10 (0.80-1.40)", "0.90 (0.60-1.20)", "1.00 (0.75-1.25)"
  )
)
forrest(dat2,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  ref_line   = 1,
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  header     = "Study",
  cols       = c("OR (95% CI)" = "or_ci")
)

title: forrest
text:
Draws a publication-ready forest plot from a data frame. Each row represents one study, subgroup, or estimate. Rows whose estimate is NA are treated as spacer/header rows: they produce no point or confidence interval, and their label is rendered in bold if non-empty.



forrest(
  data,
  estimate,
  lower,
  upper,
  label = NULL,
  group = NULL,
  is_summary = NULL,
  weight = NULL,
  ref_line = 0,
  log_scale = FALSE,
  xlim = NULL,
  xlab = "Estimate (95% CI)",
  title = NULL,
  header = NULL,
  cols = NULL,
  widths = NULL,
  pch = 15,
  lwd = 2,
  cex = 1,
  col = NULL,
  legend_pos = "topright",
  ...
)







data


A data frame, tibble, or data.table.




estimate


Column name (string) for point estimates.




lower


Column name (string) for lower confidence interval bounds.




upper


Column name (string) for upper confidence interval bounds.




label


Column name (string) for row labels displayed on the y-axis or in the left text panel. If NULL, row numbers are used.




group


Column name (string) for a grouping variable. Rows that share a group value receive the same color, and a legend is drawn automatically.




is_summary


Column name (string) of a logical vector. Rows where this is TRUE are drawn as filled diamonds (pooled / summary estimates) rather than squares with whiskers.




weight


Column name (string) of numeric study weights (e.g. inverse-variance weights or effective sample sizes). When provided, point size scales as cex * sqrt(weight / max(weight)), so larger studies are displayed with a bigger marker ‚ÄĒ the conventional meta-analysis convention. Weights for summary rows are ignored (diamond size is controlled by cex).




ref_line


Numeric. Position of the vertical reference line (e.g.¬†0 for log-odds / risk differences, 1 for odds ratios on the natural scale). Set to NULL to suppress. Default is 0.




log_scale


Logical. If TRUE, apply a log transformation to the x-axis. Useful when plotting odds ratios, hazard ratios, or risk ratios on the natural scale. Default is FALSE.




xlim


Numeric vector of length 2 giving x-axis limits. Computed from the data when NULL (default). Confidence intervals that extend beyond xlim are clipped at the axis boundary and an arrow is drawn to indicate truncation.




xlab


Label for the x-axis. Default is ‚ÄúEstimate (95% CI)‚ÄĚ.




title


Plot title. Default is NULL (no title).




header


Optional header string placed above the label column. When cols is provided this appears above the left text panel; otherwise it is drawn above the topmost row on the y-axis.




cols


Named character vector specifying extra text columns to display to the right of the plot. Names become column headers; values are column names in data. Example: cols = c(‚ÄúOR (95% CI)‚ÄĚ = ‚Äúor_text‚ÄĚ, ‚ÄúP‚ÄĚ = ‚Äúpval_text‚ÄĚ).




widths


Numeric vector of relative panel widths. When cols is NULL, ignored. Otherwise, length must equal 2 + length(cols): label panel, plot panel, then one entry per extra column. Sensible defaults are chosen automatically.




pch


Point character for non-summary rows. Default is 15 (filled square), which is conventional for forest plots.




lwd


Line width for confidence interval whiskers. Default is 2.




cex


Point size multiplier. Default is 1.




col


Color or character vector of colors. When NULL (default) and group is specified, the Okabe-Ito colorblind-safe palette is used. When NULL and no group, a single dark color is used.




legend_pos


Position of the legend when group is supplied. Passed to legend(). Set to NULL to suppress the legend. Default is ‚Äútopright‚ÄĚ.




‚Ä¶


Additional graphical parameters passed to the internal tinyplot::tinyplot() call that sets up the main plot area (e.g. cex.axis, cex.lab, font.main).






Invisibly returns NULL. Called for its side effect of producing a plot.




library("forrest")

# Basic forest plot
dat <- data.frame(
  label    = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Pooled"),
  estimate = c(-0.15, 0.10, -0.05, -0.05),
  lower    = c(-0.40, -0.10, -0.25, -0.20),
  upper    = c(0.10, 0.30, 0.15, 0.10),
  is_sum   = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)
forrest(dat,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum"
)







# With subgroup headers (NA estimate rows) and text columns
dat2 <- data.frame(
  label    = c(
    "Subgroup A", "Study 1", "Study 2", "Pooled A",
    "", "Subgroup B", "Study 3", "Study 4", "Pooled B"
  ),
  estimate = c(NA, 0.5, 0.7, 0.6, NA, NA, 1.1, 0.9, 1.0),
  lower    = c(NA, 0.3, 0.5, 0.45, NA, NA, 0.8, 0.6, 0.75),
  upper    = c(NA, 0.7, 0.9, 0.75, NA, NA, 1.4, 1.2, 1.25),
  is_sum   = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE),
  or_ci    = c(
    "", "0.50 (0.30-0.70)", "0.70 (0.50-0.90)", "0.60 (0.45-0.75)",
    "", "", "1.10 (0.80-1.40)", "0.90 (0.60-1.20)", "1.00 (0.75-1.25)"
  )
)
forrest(dat2,
  estimate   = "estimate",
  lower      = "lower",
  upper      = "upper",
  label      = "label",
  is_summary = "is_sum",
  ref_line   = 1,
  log_scale  = TRUE,
  header     = "Study",
  cols       = c("OR (95% CI)" = "or_ci")
)

title: Citation
text:
Citation
To cite package ‚Äėforrest‚Äô in publications use:

Fabbri L (2026). forrest: Publication-Ready Forest Plots. R package version 0.1.0, https://github.com/lorenzoFabbri/forrest.
